coredata.dc:title: The role of GQDs additive in TiO2 nanorods as an electron transfer layer on performance improvement of the perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Khorshidi, Elahe
Rezaei, Behzad
Irannejad, Neda
Adhami, Siavash
Ebrahimi, Marzieh
Kermanpur, Ahmad
Ensafi, Ali Asghar
coredata.dc:description: The electron transport layer (ETL) plays an important role in improving the performance of the perovskite solar cells (PSCs). The main focus is on different types of TiO2 structures as an efficient ETL. In the present work, TiO2 nanorods (NRs) are prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method and employ as an ETL in PSCs. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the TiO2 NRs based PSCs is improved about 217% compared with the mesoporous (mp) TiO2 based cells. In order to further enhance PCE of the PSCs, a new approach is adopted to synthesize the graphene quantum dots- TiO2 NRs (GQDs- TiO2 NRs) by hydrothermal method. The PCE of PSCs based on GQDs- TiO2 NRs show improvement of 1.34 times compared to the TiO2 NRs. The GQDs- TiO2 NRs is capable of being a promising alternative to be employed as an efficient electron transport materials for photovoltaic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
TiO2 nanorods
Hydrothermal synthesis
Perovskite solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the TiO2 NRs on the FTO
Synthesis of the GQDs-TiO2 NRs on the FTO
Preparation of the mp TiO2 on the FTO
Optimizing the hydrothermal synthesis variables TiO2 NRs
Effect of GQDs on TiO2 NRs structure
Optimizing procedure in photovoltaic performance point of view
PCE enhancement of prepared ETL in PSCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top view FE-SEM images of synthesized TiO2 NRs with 600 μL of TTIP in 4 h at of 160 (a), 180 (b) and 200 °C (c), and synthesized TiO2 NRs with 600 μL of TTIP at 180 °C in 3 (d), 4 (e) and 5 h (f), synthesized TiO2 NRs at 180 °C in 4 h with 400 (g), 600 (h) and 800 μL (i) of TTIP.
Top view FE-SEM images of synthesized GQDs- TiO2 NRs at 180 °C for 4 h in 600 μL of TTIP with 1 (a), 3 (b), and 5% (c) of GQDs.

UV–Vis spectra of synthesized TiO2 NRs with 600 μL of TTIP in 4 h at 160, 180 and 200 °C (a), synthesized TiO2 NRs with 600 μL of TTIP at 180 °C in 3, 4 and 5 h (b), synthesized TiO2 NRs at 180 °C in 4 h with 400, 600 and 800 μL of TTIP (c), and synthesized GQDs- TiO2 NRs with 600 μL of TTIP in 4 h at 180 °C with 1, 3 and 5% of GQDs (d).

Cross section images of the fabricated PSCs wich were based on TiO2 NRs at 180 °C for 4 h in 600 μL of TTIP as ETL(a), and fabricated PSCs which were based on GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs at 180 °C for 4 h in 600 μL of TTIP with the 3% of GQDs as ETL(b).

J-V curves of the PSCs which were based on TiO2 NRs with 600 μL of TTIP in 4 h at 160, 180 and 200 °C (a), TiO2 NRs at 180 °C with 600 μL of TTIP in 3, 4 and 5 h (b), TiO2 NRs at 180 °C in 4 h with 400, 600 and 800 μL of TTIP (c), and GQDs- TiO2 NRs at 180 °C in 4 h in 600 μL of TTIP with 1, 3 and 5% of GQDs as ETL.

UV–Vis spectra of perovskite film coated on mp- TiO2, TiO2 NRs, and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs as ETL(a), J-V curves of the PSCs which were based on mp- TiO2, TiO2 NRs, and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs as ETL(b), Nyquist plots of PSCs which were based on mp- TiO2, TiO2 NRs, and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs as ETL and the devices were measured at the bias −0.8 V under dark conditions (c).
Box plots of PSCs which were based on mp- TiO2, TiO2 NRs, and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs as ETL. for VOC (a), JSC (b), FF (C), and PCE distributions (d).
Photovoltaic performance parameters of the PSCs which were based on mp- TiO2, TiO2 NRs, and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs as ETL.
Interfacial recombination resistance (Rrec) and charge transfer resistant (Rct) extracted from the Nyquist plots of the of PSCs which were based on mp- TiO2, TiO2 NRs, and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs as ETL.

head.ce:title: The role of GQDs additive in TiO2 nanorods as an electron transfer layer on performance improvement of the perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156-83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electron transport layer (ETL) plays an important role in improving the performance of the perovskite solar cells (PSCs). The main focus is on different types of TiO2 structures as an efficient ETL. In the present work, TiO2 nanorods (NRs) are prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method and employ as an ETL in PSCs. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the TiO2 NRs based PSCs is improved about 217% compared with the mesoporous (mp) TiO2 based cells. In order to further enhance PCE of the PSCs, a new approach is adopted to synthesize the graphene quantum dots- TiO2 NRs (GQDs- TiO2 NRs) by hydrothermal method. The PCE of PSCs based on GQDs- TiO2 NRs show improvement of 1.34 times compared to the TiO2 NRs. The GQDs- TiO2 NRs is capable of being a promising alternative to be employed as an efficient electron transport materials for photovoltaic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the first research in sensitized solar cells by Miyasaka on organo-lead halide perovskites, a new vision was developed for the next-generation of solar cells by employing organometallic halide perovskite materials [1–3]. These materials have the general formula of ABX3 where A is a representative for the organics, which can be CH3NH3 +, NH2CHNH2 +, CH3CH2NH3 +, B is a sign for the metallic part like Pb2+, Sn2+, and finally, X acts as a symbol of halides such as Cl−, Br− or I−. The materials with a such structure is taught to be a very good choice among the light absorbent materials due to their outstanding optoelectronic properties such as high optical absorption coefficient, direct optical bandgap, long carrier diffusion lengths (≈1 μm range), ambipolar transport, and the capability of changing and tuning the bandgap energies from 1.1 to 2.3 eV by cation, and/or halides exchange [4–6]. In a typical perovskite solar cell, the perovskite layer is sandwiched between a metal oxide layer, with either mp or compact structure as an ETL, and a p-type semiconductor as hole transport layer (HTL) [7,8]. The rapid progress, achieved in recent years, has brought the efficiency of perovskite solar cells up to 22% [9]. The ETL nanostructures improve the electron transfer phenomenon and also provide mechanical support and scaffold for the perovskite [10].

The most common transparent semiconducting metal oxides, which are compatible with the perovskite layer, are TiO2, SnO2, ZnO, SrTiO3 and WO3. These compounds are capable of transferring the photogenerated electrons to the substrate and blocking the holes simultaneously [11,12]. In this regard, one of the most significant and effective materials is mp- TiO2 related to its mp architecture which consists of spherical nanoparticles with an approximate size of 20 nm. However, the created grain boundaries restrict the electrons flow within the structure, causing poor performance for the solar cell [13]. Another important parameter, which must be considered, is the increment of recombination’s probability due to the random walk of electrons through the ETL [14]. This issue can be overcome with the change in the ETL structure from mp to NRs. NRs are capable of providing a direct path, high internal surface area, and lower charge transfer resistance and therefore, offering a faster transport of electrons and the separation of charge carriers [15,16]. The TiO2 NRs can achieve a tremendous yield of 18% when competing with all other NRs and nanotubes used as an ETL (ZnO, WO3, and CdS) in perovskite solar cells [17–20]. When the performance of the perovskite solar cell is concerned, it is clear that the precise surface engineering of ETL plays an important role, in which superior electron mobility, conductivity to attenuate excessive charge accumulation and guarantee of effective electron injection, can be achieved [21].

Nowadays, GQDs are known as a new class of carbon compounds that have attracted much attention due to their discrete, quasi-spherical nanoparticles and particle size of less than 10 nm [22,23]. GQDs have become suitable choice as a GQDs/semiconductor composite, due to their excellent optical-electrical properties, broad absorption spectrum, a variation of emission wavelength, multiple exciton generations, high photostability, easy modification, and their compatibility with the environment [24–26]. The high electrical conductivity and lower electron traps of the GQDs help to perform as an effective electron acceptor for semiconductor nanostructure, facilitating the carrier separations, and therefore increasing the PCE of PSCs [27,28]. It is worth noting the upconversion effect of GQDs, where they can provide more opportunities to convert NIR light into the visible range. This phenomenon promotes the photogeneration of the electrons and increases the photovoltaic performance of PSCs [29].
Considering the mentioned aspects, it seems to be a wise approach to employ the GQDs in semiconductor nanostructures for photovoltaic applications.

There are few reports in litrature about the employing of graphene quantum dots in ETL [27,30,31]. whereas, this works is focused specifically on the improvements in electrical properties of ETL and the effect of GQDs on the morphology of ETL has not been described in details. In this work for the first time, a simple one-step hydrothermal method was applied for direct synthesis of TiO2 NRs on the substrate to be employed as the ETL layer in PSCs. The NRs were optimized, in different aspects including the dimensions and morphologies, by adjusting the hydrothermal conditions. The established parameters were employed in the further section of the work in which, a new approach was adopted to synthesize the GQDs- TiO2 NRs by hydrothermal method. The performance of the photovoltaic devices was then investigated in different aspects. Considering the 1-D structures of the NRs, the combination of higher electron mobility and the upconversion effect of GQDs enhanced the electron transfer rate, increased photogeneration of electrons, reduced the charge recombination, and therefore increased JSC, VOC, fill factor (FF) and subsequently PCE of the devices.

Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) sheets and TiO2 paste were purchased from Solaronix. Titanium (IV)-isopropoxide (TTIP 97%), methylammonium bromide (MABr, >99.5%), formamidinium iodide (FAI, >99.5%), cesium iodide, lead(II) iodide (PbI2,99%), lead(II) bromide (PbBr2, 99%), N2,N2,N2′,N2′,N7,N7,N7′,N7′-octakis(4-methoxyphenyl)-9,9′-spirobi[9H-fluorene]-2,2′,7,7′-tetramine (Spiro-OMeTAD, 99%), 4-tert-butylpyridine (TBP, 96%), lithium bis (trimethylsilyl) amide (LiTFSI, 97%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), anhydrous chlorobenzene (CB, 99.8%), anhydrous ethanol, anhydrous dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), anhydrous N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), citric acid monohydrate (CA), diethylenetriamine (DETA) were all supplied from Sigma-Aldrich Company. All the chemicals and reagents are directly used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the TiO2 NRs on the FTO
ce:section.ce:para: A sheet of FTO was selected and cut into squares (1.4 × 1.4 mm) to be used as a substrate. The specimens were etched by Zn powder and HCl solution, followed by cleaning process in each of the followings individually dionized (DI) water, HCl 0.1 M, ethanol and acetone for 15 min in an ultrasonic bath. The fabrication process was performed by mixing 25 mL DI water and 25 mL HCl (37%) and stirring for 5 min at the room temperature while a certain amount of TTIP was adding into the mixture. The adding amount were 400, 600 and 800 μL. It is then poured into the designed autoclave vessel (100 mL) over the prepared FTO substrates; it was sealed and placed in an electric oven at different conditions to conduct the hydrothermal growth of NRs. The conditions were varied to find the optimized conditions; the temperature from 160 to 200 °C and time schedule of 3–5 h. Finally, the autoclave was cooled down to ambient condition; the contents were rinsed with DI water and dried under nitrogen flow. Eventually, TiO2 NRs were annealed at 500 °C for 30 min [32].

In order to prepare the GQDs, the following approach was adopted; 1 mmol CA (0.21 g), 3 mmol DETA (0.34 g), and 5 mL H2O were mixed in the autoclave and stirred until form a clear solution. The sealed autoclave vessel was then placed in an electric oven, set at 180 °C and hold for 6 h. Eventually, the solution was washed by abundant ethanol and centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 min [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the GQDs-TiO2 NRs on the FTO
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs- TiO2 NRs were synthesized following the same procedure as the TiO2 NRs, but with a different content ratio of GQDs in the stirring solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the mp TiO2 on the FTO
ce:section.ce:para: The diluted TiO2 paste in anhydrous ethanol (2:7, weight ratio) was deposited on FTO by spin coating at the rate of 5000 rpm for 25 s; the specimens were undergone annealing process at 500 °C for 30 min [34].

The Spiro-OMeTAD solution, as a HTL, was prepared by dissolving 72.3 mg of Spiro-OMeTAD in 1 mL of CB, 17.5 mL of LiTFSI stock solution (520 mg of LiTFSI in 1.0 mL of acetonitrile) as well as 28.8 mL of TBT as a dopant.

In order to prepare perovskite solution, the certain amount of FAI (171.97 mg), PbI2 (507.11 mg), MABr (22.39 mg), PbBr2 (73.40 mg) were dissolved in 1.0 mL of DMF/DMSO (4:1) and stirred for 1 h at the ambient temperature and accomplished by adding 59 μL of CsI in DMSO (1.3 × 10−3 M) [35].

The prepared substrates, including FTO/TiO2 NRs, FTO/GQDs- TiO2 NRs and FTO/mp-TiO2, were transferred into a glovebox with N2 controlled atmosphere. The perovskite film was deposited on the ETL by two-step spin coating procedure based on the antisolvent addition: first at 1000 rpm for 10 s and then at 6000 rpm for 20 s. At the middle of the second step, 100 μL of CB was dropped on the spinning substrate to obtain dense, pinhole-free perovskite film. Substrates were then annealed at 100 °C for 50 min. Then, Spiro-OMeTAD solution was spin coated on top of perovskite film at 4000 rpm for 20 s. Finally, a thin layer of Au electrode was deposited by thermal evaporation method at a high vacuum; the complete devices were made.

The J-V characteristic curves of the PSCs were calculated by Biologic SP-300 potentiostat under one sun illumination AM 1.5 G simulated sunlight with a power density of 100 mW cm−2 in ambient. The light intensity of the solar simulator was calibrated by a Si reference cell. A double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer Jasco V-750, was used to obtain absorbance spectra of the ETL and perovskite films. The EIS measurements were carried out by the Eco-Chemie Autolab PGSTAT 302N electrochemical workstation, and the EIS spectra were processed by Nova 1.11 software. The FE-SEM (JSM-7001) at an acceleration voltage of 5 kV was employed to assess the surface morphologies of the ETL, perovskite films and cross sectional images. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was also achieved using a Philips XL30 instrument. The Grazing incident X-ray diffraction (GIXRD) patterns were recorded by Philips X Pert PRO MPD diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.542 Å) under operation condition of 20 kV and 30 mA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the hydrothermal synthesis variables TiO2 NRs
ce:section.ce:para: It is very important to optimize the parameters of hydrothermal synthesis (time, temperature, and concentration of the Ti precursor). This is to determine the most appropriate morphology of TiO2 NRs as an efficient ETL. In order to assess the optimized morphology of TiO2 NRs, different parameters were considered and adjusted including time, temperature, and concentration of Ti precursor. Morphology of TiO2 NRs plays an important role in the photovoltaic performance of the final PSCs. TiO2 NRs with a short length and low density do not offer a favorable morphology for charge separation because of the low surface area and low roughness factor. Also, the large inter-rods spacing between NRs do not create a uniform coating on the FTO, and therefore the probability of recombining the charge carriers increases due to the direct contact of the ETL (TiO2 NRs) with the perovskite layer. On the other hand, long and high densely TiO2 NRs with low inter-rods space provide poor infiltration of perovskite in TiO2 NRs. Thus, the transport path of the photogenerated electrons from the perovskite to TiO2 NRs increase, and so accelerate the recombination rate of the charge carrier. Therefore, in order to provide a large surface area and an inter-rods space with appropriate penetration of the perovskite, the morphology of the TiO2 NRs must be optimized. Fig. 1 (a–c) shows the top view FE-SEM micrographs of the synthesized TiO2 NRs at temperatures of 160, 180 and 200 °C, for 4 h in 600 μL of TTIP. At a lower temperature, 160 °C, the growth of TiO2 NRs occurred in the thermodynamically preferred orientations; although the coverage was not satisfactory. It was not dense enough to coat the FTO surface. This can be related to the kinetics of the reaction where low nucleation and growth rate occurred due to the slow reaction rate. It is clear that the hydrolysis of TTIP was directly related to the reaction temperature; therefore, by increasing the temperature to 180 °C, NRs became denser and more homogeneous. However, at the temperature above 180 °C, a very dense layer of NRs formed which were considerably bigger in terms of length and diameter resulting in a very low inter-rods space in which, the perovskite crystals cannot penetrate between the NRs. Therefore, the most suitable temperature was established to be 180 °C for this process.

In order to investigate the effect of reaction time on the morphology of TiO2 NRs, the same procedure as before was adopted for 3, 4 and 5 h at the established temperature. Fig. 1d-f shows the FE-SEM micrographs of the synthesized TiO2 NRs for 3, 4 and 5 h reaction times. As can be seen, when the reaction time was 3 h, the formed NRs were relatively smaller in size and not dense enough to cover the FTO surface. It can be related to insufficient growth time. Whereas the NRs dimensions (length and diameter) increased when the longer reaction time was given. Consequently, a denser morphology was obtained on the FTO surface. Given a longer time (5 h), a very dense layer of TiO2 NRs was achieved. In this case, the tight arrangement was observed causing a very limited inter-rods space. Therefore, it can be concluded that the most appropriate reaction time to be 4 h.

Having established the optimized reaction temperature and duration, it was mandatory to adjust the TTIP concentration as well. The FE-SEM micrographs of the TiO2 NRs, grown on the FTO surface at 180 °C and 4 h in 400, 600 and 800 μL of TTIP which are shown in Fig. 1g-i. As it can be seen, at a lower concentration TiO2 NRs were spotted on the surface; at the higher concentrations, the presence of the NRs was more obvious.

As it was expected, at the highest concentration, NRs became denser and therefore, very limited inter-rods spaces were available thus, the perovskite infiltration will be highly affected. This can be associated with the high nucleation density, in which the diagonally grown NRs collide with the adjacent and adopt a vertical direction. However, when the nucleation density is low it is very likely for the NRs to grow at an angle to the substrate surface normal [36,37]. Hence, it was verified that a suitable concentration to be 400 μL. The length and diameter of TiO2 NRs in different hydrothermal synthesis variables are measured and shown in Table S1.

It was clearly shown that hydrothermal synthesis of TiO2 NRs at 180 °C for 4 h in 400 μL of TTIP as Ti pressure provided appropriate morphology of TiO2 NRs to be employed as an efficient ETL. It is worth to remind that the improvement process was accomplished by employing GQDs as a dopant. This issue is discussed in details in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQDs on TiO2 NRs structure
ce:section.ce:para: The investigation was carried out following the same approach as before, different amounts of GQDs (1, 3, and 5% V/V) were added in the autoclave vessel. The FE-SEM micrographs are shown in Fig. 2 (a–c). The GQDs- TiO2 NRs can be observed on the surface scattered across the FTO substrate. They are thought to be capable of improving the perovskite infiltration compared to the TiO2 NRs. By increasing the content ratio of the GQDs (from 1 to 3%), a decrement was observed in case of the NRs dimension, although the density of NRs increased. It is very likely that GQDs were capable of reducing the NRs growth rate while their density increased. Elevating the content ratio of GQDs to 5%, there was no difference observed in term of the NRs dimensions despite the density which becomes lower. Hence, a further increment was not really appropriate where the improvement of NRs morphology was concerned.
The length and diameter of GQDs- TiO2 NRs in different content of GQDs are measured and shown in Table S2.

The synthesized GQDs was characterized by FT-IR. The characteristic signs of carbonyl and amine groups can be distinguished in the spectrum. The presence of these groups increase the probability of the interaction between GQDs and TiO2 NRs. A number of interactions are thought to be in charge during the adsorption phenomena between the carbonyl and amine groups from GQDs and TiO2 surface including hydrogen bonding and donor-acceptor [38,39]. Therefore, hydrothermal synthesis leads to an efficient binding between GQDs with TiO2 by carbonyl and amine groups of GQDs.
The optimized amount of the additive was determined to be 3% in the hydrothermal synthesis of TiO2 NRs, however, the presence of GQDs on the NRs were needed to be verified and confirmed.

Fig. S1 presents the EDX spectra of the TiO2 NRs and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs. All the characteristic peaks, associated with the contributed materials, can be clearly observed. Presence of C is related to the GQDs in GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs.

Morphology of the perovskite film coated on the TiO2 NRs and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs, were analyzed with FE-SEM (Fig. S2). The micrographs show the uniform perovskite crystals with similar size (up to 250 nm) and structure throughout the substrate.

Optical properties of the synthesized TiO2 NRs in different hydrothermal conditions were investigated by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis absorption of synthesized TiO2 NRs with 600 μL of TTIP at the temperatures of 160, 180 and 200 °C in 4 h. Synthesized TiO2 NRs in 160 °C exhibit a maximum absorption peak in the 320 nm, which is attributed to TiO2. The absorption intensity seemed to be directly related to the synthesis temperature, in which, by increasing the temperature a shift appeared to the graph toward the visible range together with an increment in the absorption intensity. Fig. 3b presents UV–Vis absorption of synthesized TiO2 NRs with 600 μL of TTIP at the temperatures of 180 °C at 3, 4 and 5 h. As shown, there is an increasing tendency of absorption intensity and shift to the visible region with increasing hydrothermal synthesis time. Fig. 3c shows UV–Vis absorption of synthesized TiO2 NRs at temperatures of 180 °C with 400, 600 and 800 μL of TTIP. Synthesized TiO2 NRs with 400 μL of TTIP shows a weak absorption peak in 310 nm. With an increase in the concentration of TTIP (thicker layer is formed) absorption shifts to the visible region together with an increase in the absorption intensity. The obtained results also confirm that the synthesized TiO2 NRs in 600 μL of TTIP at 180 °C within 4 h have the appropriate optical properties as ETL in PSCs.

Fig. 3d shows UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs- TiO2 NRs with the 1, 3, and 5% of GQDs with the adjusted hydrothermal conditions. The absorption peak of GQDs- TiO2 NRs exhibits a blue shift along with a decrease in the absorbance intensity compared with the TiO2 NRs. This can be related to the size of the TiO2 NRs which is dominated by the GQDs content. Therefore, the size of NRs lead to a size-related shifts in absorbance spectra which can be either toward blue or red shift depending on decrement or increment of the NRs, respectively [40,41].

There is a decreased tendency of absorption with increasing GQDs content in the TiO2 NRs. while both cases display poor absorption in the visible region (400–800 nm), GQDs- TiO2 NRs show less absorption that allows more incident light to reach the perovskite film and thus increases harvesting light efficiency, which is the reason for higher JSC from GQDs- TiO2 NRs. The obtained data was in total agreement with the obtained results from the J-V curves.

The presence of GQDs in the ETL structure was also approved by XRD (Fig. S3). As it can be seen in Fig. S3, the (002) peak is discerned associated with GQDs, as observed in previous works [42–44]. Comparing the spectra in Fig. S3 highlights the difference between the intensities of the perovskite related peaks indicating the effect of GQDs on the crystallinity of the perovskite film, which is in a good agreement with the corresponding FE-SEM images and UV–Vis spectroscopy results as shown previously.

Fig. 4 displays a cross sectional images of the fabricated PSCs based on TiO2 NRs and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs. Comparing the images shows how perovskite fully infiltrated in the inter-rods spacing of NRs and there was no accumulation of perovskite on top of them. The thickness of this layer is approximately 500 nm. This architecture increases the contact of perovskite and NRs and thus, improves electron transfer, reduces the recombination of electrons and holes and subsequently enhances PCE. The Spiro-OMeTAD film is uniformly deposited over the perovskite with a thickness of 100 nm. The thickness of the Au film is 90 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing procedure in photovoltaic performance point of view

ce:section.ce:para: Eventually, the photovoltaic performance of PSCs based on the synthesized TiO2 NRs in the different hydrothermal conditions was assessed by J-V measurements under AM 1.5 G solar illumination at the 100 mW cm−2.

Fig. 5 a shows the J-V curves of fabricated PSCs (six cells) in the different temperature and attributing photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table S3. Once again, the poor performance of PSCs, based on synthesized TiO2 NRs at 160 °C, was attributed to low distribution of TiO2 NRs on the FTO surface which did not really offer a good coverage of the surface area and leading to charge recombination phenomena, due to the contact of FTO substrate and perovskite film, and therefore, causing the lower JSC and FF. The average PCE of PSCs exhibited a drop from 8.76 to 4.38% for TiO2 NRs synthesized in 200 °C in compare to the synthesized NRs at 180 °C. This result can be assigned to the rapid growth of TiO2 NRs with larger dimensions on the FTO surface due to the high synthesis temperature. The denser and longer TiO2 NRs prevent the penetration of perovskite into NRs inter-rods space causing poor contact between NRs and perovskite, which decrease the charge collection and separation. Also, the thickened TiO2 NRs decreases the light absorption and convicting more probability of charge recombination during the long pathway of electron transfer. All the mention aspects can affect photovoltaic performance.

The J-V curves of the PSCs, based on the synthesized TiO2 NRs, were also obtained in the different time intervals of hydrothermal synthesis (Fig. 5b, Table S4). The average PCE of PSCs increases from 5.67% for the one based on TiO2 NRs in 3 h, to 8.76% for those in 4 h, while this value decreases to 6.02% for the devices with TiO2 NRs synthesized in 5 h. This trend is very similar to that of the PSCs based on the synthesized TiO2 NRs in the different temperatures of hydrothermal synthesis. This phenomenon can be related to the density and surface area of TiO2, which are relatively low in 3 h. In case of a longer synthesis time (5 h), the parameters under investigation have larger dimensions concluding a lower inter-rods spaces of TiO2 NRs. These are the reasons for the reduction of photovoltaic performance of the fabricated devices when the NRs were synthesized in 3 and 5 h compared to those made in 4 h.

Fig. 5c shows J-V curves of PSCs based on synthesized TiO2 NRs in different content of TTIP and attributing photovoltaic parameters are recorded in Table S5. As shown in Fig. 5c, increasing the content of TTIP from 400 to 600 μL, leads to an increase of the average PCE from 4.50 to 8.76%. The elevation is due to the increase of density and distribution of TiO2 NRs on the FTO surface. High density and distribution of NRs facilitate the collection and transfer of electron and prevent the direct contact between FTO substrate and perovskite film, and therefore improvement of JSC, FF and subsequently average PCE. However, with increasing the content of TTIP from 600 to 800 μL, the inter-rods space and infiltration of perovskite decrease, resulting in a decrease of contact between the NRs and perovskite film. Also, the light absorption by perovskite film decreases due to the expansion of TiO2 NRs. Thus, PSCs based on synthesized TiO2 NRs with 800 μL of TTIP exhibited low JSC, VOC, FF and finally poor photovoltaic performance compared to PSCs based on synthesized TiO2 NRs in 600 μL of TTIP. Perhaps, regarding the mentioned achievements, the best results can be awarded to the PSCs based on synthesized TiO2 NRs at 180 °C in 600 μL of TTIP within 4 h, in which, the appropriate dimension and surface morphology can facilitate the interface contact between the perovskite and ETL, and therefore elevate the charge transfer rate and reduce the charge recombination.

In order to improve the photovoltaic performance of the PSCs, it was thought to employ additive to modify the ETL properties. Therefore, GQDs were inserted into the optimized TiO2 NRs. However, it was necessary to adjust the right amount of the mentioned additive and characterize the developed ETL structure.

Therefore, GQDs- TiO2 NRs with 1, 3, and 5% of GQDs were prepared. The obtained results are shown in Table S6 and Fig. 5d. All the PSCs based on GQDs- TiO2 NRs exhibited a remarkable improvement in the average PCE compared to those based on TiO2 NRs. When GQDs content increased from 1 to 3%, the average PCE increased from 11.30 to 11.74%, presumably, due to an increase in inter-rods space of the NRs and thus better infiltration of perovskite into NRs. While, the further increment of the GQDs to 5% was not beneficial for the average PCE, and it showed a drop from 11.74 to 9.10%. This result can be attributed to low density and distribution of NRs on the FTO surface. Therefore, the greatest PCE of 11.74% was verified to be obtained by GQDs- TiO2 NRs with 3% of additive.

In order to assess the effect of the GQDs, EIS was carried out to determine the charge carrier transport and recombination behavior of the PSCs based GQDs- TiO2 NRs with various contents of the additive. Fig. S4 presents the Nyquist plots for those devices in the frequency range of 1 MHz–10 mHz at a bias −0.8 V, and attributing fitted data by an equivalent circuit were listed in Table S7. As shown in the equivalent circuit, the internal series resistance, Rs, is related to sheet resistance of the FTO glass and contact resistance of the cell, the charge transfer resistance, Rct, refers to the contact resistance across the interface and Rrec assigns the charge recombination resistance of the electron in the TiO2 and perovskite. There are two separated irregular semicircles, in the Nyquist plots. The small semicircle, in the high frequency region, was corresponded to charge transport resistance (Rct) and large semicircle, in the low frequency region, was attributed to the recombination resistance (Rrec) [45,46].

When the content of GQDs elevated, from 1 to 3%, Rrec increased from 634 to 840 kΩ and subsequently, Rct decreased from 35.0 to 27.0 kΩ. The collected information highlights the ability of GQDs in improving the electron transfer and suppressing the charge recombination across the ETL/perovskite interface. On the other hand, by increasing the GQDs from 3 to 5%, a drop was observed in term of Rrec, from 840 to 660 kΩ. Meanwhile, Rct was increased from 27.0 to 41.0 kΩ. This can be interpreted by considering the charge separation across the ETL/perovskite interface due to the reduced density of TiO2 NRs.

So far, the achievements were focused on the optimizing procedures; although it was necessary to investigate the effect of TiO2 NRs and GQDs on the photovoltaic performance of PSCs, also to show how well improved the properties and discuss the facts in details. Therefore, a PSC was fabricated based on mp-TiO2 and used as a reference cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PCE enhancement of prepared ETL in PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Employing the obtained achievements and applying the adjusted parameters were accomplished by assessing the samples (mp-TiO2/perovskite, TiO2 NRs/perovskite and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs/perovskite) using the UV–Vis (Fig. 6 a). A similar absorption profile was observed with an absorption peak for perovskite in wavelength of 750 nm. However, it is obvious that perovskite film on the GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs had a higher absorption over all the wavelength range in the spectrum. The enhanced absorption of perovskite on the TiO2 NRs, compared to the perovskite on the mp- TiO2, was observed due to the change in the structure in term of crystallinity. Whereas, the improved absorption in perovskite on the GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs was due to the upconversion effect of GQDs which absorbs the NIR light and emit the photons in the visible wavelength range. The latter perfectly suits the perovskite where the expansion of the light absorption increases the photogenerated electrons and subsequently increment JSC.

The photovoltaic performance of the PSCs fabricated based on mp- TiO2, NRs TiO2, and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs was recorded by J-V measurement under AM 1.5 G solar illumination at the 100 mW cm−2. Fig. 6b shows the J-V curves of these cells; corresponding photovoltaic parameters are summarized in Table 1 . As can be seen, by changing the TiO2 structure from mp to NRs, the average PCE was dramatically improved (217%) due to the special morphology of TiO2 NRs. It is thought that TiO2 NRs have fewer defects and provide the direct paths for electron transfer from perovskite to ETL and then to FTO substrate; therefore the electron transfer is being improved and charge recombination reduced. Also, infiltration of perovskite into inter-rods space of TiO2 NRs, increases the direct contact between perovskite and ETL which can be strongly helpful in terms of extracting and collecting the electrons. On the other hand, in the case of mp- TiO2 structure, it seems that the grain boundaries between particle aggregates may act as a defect and become the centers of electron trapping. Considering the mentioned facts regarding the ETL structure, PSCs based on TiO2 NRs exhibits a remarkable improvement in JSC, VOC, FF and subsequently PCE.

It was established that the presence of GQDs can significantly improve the PCE (34% compared to the TiO2 NRs without GQDs). In this case, the NRs were sparsely formed in which, an appropriate inter-rods space were available for better penetration of perovskite into NRs. These properties were capable of establishing direct contacts between ETL and perovskite. It is worth noting that the GQDs can improve the electrical conductivity of the ETL due to the excellent charge mobility where they do hardly behave as a trap for electrons. Moreover, GQDs can act as an electron acceptor same as fullerenes, and thus provide an efficient channel for charge collection and fast pathway for electron transport from perovskite to ETL [47,48]. Another considerable capability, regarding the GQDs, is the states of the π orbitals which are widely distributed across the surface and act as very fast electron funnel [49]. It is very likely that the GQDs also contribute to form a weak electronic bonding between the perovskite and ETL causing a better charge injection [26,45]. On the other hand, GQDs pose the upconversion effect, whereas they can absorb NIR light and emit visible light. This is a marvelous specification which enhances the light capture ability and increases the photogenerated electrons and subsequently increases JSC. Undoubtedly, this specification is very helpful to improve the electron collection and reduce the charge recombination and providing higher JSC, VOC, and FF.
It might be interesting to assess the behavior of the devices with modified ETL structures in terms of charge transfer and interfacial charge recombination influenced by GQDs (Fig. 6c, Table 2 ).

Fig. 6c indicates that Rrec for PSCs based on GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs is more than the other structures meanwhile Rct is falling off. These results approve the capability of GQDs- TiO2 NRs in terms of efficient charge collection as well as fast electron extraction from perovskite to ETL. These specifications have a significant improving effect on FF, VOC, and JSC. The clarified results from EIS are in absolute agreement with the J-V and UV–Vis results in Fig. 6a and b, respectively.

Box plots obtained from the J-V curves of the PSCs based on mp- TiO2, NRs TiO2, and GQDs 3%- TiO2 NRs of six cells shown in Fig. 7 . Eventually, a new horizon has been opened up when every stage of the work has been begun with UV–Vis spectroscopy technique and surprisingly the results were verified by other employed methods. This shows a very simple approach to contemplating new hypotheses.

The TiO2 NRs were synthesized by a simple one-step hydrothermal method and employed as an ETL in PSCs. Morphology and dimensions of TiO2 NRs were optimized through the change of temperature, time and content of TTIP as the source of Ti in the hydrothermal. Significant improvement was achieved in PCE (217%) compared with the mesoscopic TiO2 ETL. The results showed the presence of a compact layer on FTO is unnecessary when the hydrothermal method is employed. A further significant improvement was achieved in the PCE of the PSCs by the application of the hydrothermally synthesized GQDs- TiO2 NRs as ETL. This phenomenon was as a result of the optimization process which was specifically designed for this purpose leading to the determination of the most suitable amount of the GQDs. The findings confirm that the combination of TiO2 NRs and GQDs is capable of reducing the charge recombination, increasing the charge transfer, improving the optical properties of TiO2 as an ETL in the PSCs, and subsequently enhancing the PCE of PSCs. It is very likely that the present study enables the researchers across the world to become familiar with a simple, straight forward and indeed, an effective approach for improvements in the performance of the photovoltaic devices, particularly halide perovskites, when the electron transport layer is concerned.

This works is focused specifically on the improvements in electrical properties of ETL and the effect of GQDs on the morphology of ETL has not been described in details. It must be considered that in this work a new and unique study is introduced in which, the simultaneous and direct synthesis of GQD/1-D TiO2 NRs on the FTO substrate was studied by a simple hydrothermal method. On the other hand, optimization of the synthetic conditions concluded that in presence of GQDs, the morphology and optoelectronic properties of TiO2 were improved significantly. Therefore, the proposed method can be an effective approach in improving the solar cell performance concept.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the Isfahan University of Technology, the center of excellence in sensor.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2020.135822.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Renaissance of fullerenes with perovskite solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, T. Jiu, C. Duan, Y. Wang, H. Zhang, H. Jian, Y. Zhao, N. Wang, C. Huang, Y. Li, The Renaissance of fullerenes with perovskite solar cells, Nano Energy 2018, 46, 331.
